participants.extracted: Women aged 18 to 48 years with menstrual cycles 21 to 35 days who met criteria for premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD) after at least two months of prospective daily symptom charting. Eligible participants were not currently experiencing a major depressive episode or a substance use disorder other than tobacco, and had no lifetime bipolar disorder, psychotic illness, bulimia, severe suicidal thoughts, sertraline hypersensitivity, pregnancy, or lactation. Women were also excluded if they were receiving psychotropic medication or certain hormonal contraceptive or device-based treatments that could suppress menses, if they had used an oral contraceptive for less than six months before screening or could not continue the same contraceptive during the study, if they used inadequate contraception, if they were planning to relocate, or if they were unable or unwilling to provide informed consent. Participants were recruited at sites in New Haven, New York City, and Richmond; 125 were assigned to sertraline and 127 to placebo.

interventions.extracted: Participants were randomized 1:1 to symptom-onset dosing with sertraline or matching placebo for six menstrual cycles. The active intervention started at sertraline 50 mg/day (two capsules) taken once daily during the symptomatic interval, initiated when participants first noticed their typical premenstrual symptoms and stopped within a few days of menstrual flow and symptom resolution. If response was inadequate (CGI-S >2), the dose could be titrated up to 100 mg/day (four capsules) by increasing two capsules every two days and then tapered in reverse at the end of the symptomatic interval; if moderate to severe side effects occurred, the dose could be reduced to 25 mg/day (one capsule).

outcomes.extracted: Primary outcome: premenstrual symptom severity measured with the Premenstrual Tension Scale (PMTS), a 10-item scale with total score range 0–36, assessed at baseline and repeatedly during follow-up for the 7 days prior to menses across the treatment cycles. Secondary outcomes: depressive symptom severity measured with the Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology-Clinician version (IDS-C), a 28-item scale with total score range 0–84, assessed for the 7 days prior to menses across follow-up cycles; overall premenstrual symptom severity measured with the Daily Rating of Severity of Problems (DRSP), including total score and subscale scores, calculated from daily ratings over the final five days of the luteal phase and used across study cycles; global severity and global improvement measured with the Clinical Global Impression-Severity (CGI-S) and Clinical Global Impression-Improvement (CGI-I) scales, each rated on a 1–7 ordinal scale at follow-up visits; discontinuation symptoms measured with the Michelson SSRI Withdrawal Scale, summed for the three days after pill taking ended in each menstrual cycle; and DRSP subscales for depressive symptoms, physical symptoms, and anger/irritability, derived from daily ratings across cycles using the same luteal-phase scoring approach.
